behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions (e.g., month 13, day 32, malformed syntax) with a descriptive error message.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error construction in a local function/object boundary. No OS/network/subprocess/LLM dependency. A mock is unnecessary and would not reveal additional real-world failures beyond direct parser logic.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with invalid cron strings (`"0 0 * 13 *"`, `"0 0 32 * *"`, malformed token counts). Assert it returns/throws structured errors that include the invalid field and reason (not just generic failure), and that no schedule object is produced.

behaviors.description: The next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron expressions, including edge cases like "last day of month" and "nearest weekday."
behaviors.justification: Real failures often occur at module boundaries between cron parsing, semantic normalization (L/W semantics), and timestamp generation. A pure unit test of one function could miss parser-to-calculator interpretation mismatches.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end in-process from cron string -> schedule object -> next N timestamps. Verify outputs for `L` and `W` across multiple months (including leap/non-leap years) from fixed start times. Assert exact ordered timestamps and count, using real date/time library behavior (no mocked parser or calendar math).

behaviors.description: The overlap detector identifies when two schedules would fire within the configured minimum gap (e.g., 5 minutes) and reports the conflicting time windows.
behaviors.justification: Detecting real contention depends on wiring schedule generation with overlap logic and gap policy. Mocking schedule outputs could hide boundary/rounding bugs in how generated run times are consumed.
behaviors.test_description: Create two cron schedules via the real parser/calculator pipeline, generate upcoming windows, and run overlap detection with a 5-minute gap. Assert detector returns specific conflict pairs/windows (start/end or timestamp pairs), including a non-overlap control case. Keep all dependencies in-process, no external I/O.

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across a DST transition — a job scheduled for 2:30 AM handles the spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity correctly.
behaviors.justification: DST correctness depends on real timezone rules and runtime tzdata/OS behavior; mocks can hide ambiguous/nonexistent local-time handling differences. This crosses into OS/runtime infrastructure rather than pure module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Execute scheduling with real IANA timezone data (e.g., `America/New_York`) on actual runtime tz handling. For spring-forward date, assert policy for nonexistent `02:30` (skip/shift according to engine spec). For fall-back date, assert handling of duplicate `02:30` (single vs two runs per spec, with correct offsets). Validate exact timestamps and offsets around transition boundaries.

behaviors.description: When a scheduled job's computed next run time has already passed (e.g., system was down), the engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on the job's configured missed-run policy.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is policy evaluation wired with persisted scheduler state and run-time computation. A unit test of policy logic alone can miss state/clock interaction bugs during recovery.
behaviors.test_description: Using real scheduler modules plus local persistence (test DB/file store), seed a job whose next run is in the past, then simulate restart/recovery. Run once with `skip` policy and once with `backfill` policy; assert emitted execution plan (no run vs queued catch-up runs) and updated persisted next-run state.
